coredata.dc:title: Inner filter effect-modulated ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor based on competition strategy for zearalenone detection in cereal crops: Using mitoxantrone as quencher of CdTe QDs@SiO2
coredata.dc:creator: Bi, Xiaoya
Li, Libo
Liu, Xiaohong
Luo, Lijun
Cheng, Zhiliang
Sun, Jinying
Cai, Zhibin
Liu, Jinming
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description: Herein, an innovative ratiometric fluorescence (FL) aptasensor was successfully fabricated for the accurate analysis of zearalenone (ZEN) in corn and barley flour. The ZEN aptamer-modified nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs-apt) and silica sphere-encapsulated cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTe QDs@SiO2) were directly mixed and applied as ratiometric probes. In the absence of ZEN, mitoxantrone (MTX), which was innovatively introduced as quencher, was captured by NGQDs-apt and its inner filter effect (IFE) on CdTe QDs@SiO2 was inhibited. When ZEN existed, MTX separated from NGQDs-apt and re-dispersed around CdTe QDs@SiO2 owing to the competitive binding of ZEN with its aptamer. As the IFE of free MTX on CdTe QDs@SiO2 recovering, the FL intensity of CdTe QDs@SiO2 was quenched, while the FL intensity of NGQDs-apt was nearly invariant. On this basis, a ratiometric FL aptasensor for ZEN was fabricated, which exhibited outstanding detection performances with a desirable detection limit of 0.32 pg mL−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Zearalenone
Mitoxantrone
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Feasibility and mechanism investigation of the ratiometric FL system for ZEN detection
Analytical performance of the developed aptasensor for ZEN detection
Analysis of ZEN in corn and barley flour

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of NGQDs (line a), ZEN-aptamer (line b) and NGQDs-apt (line c). (B) The FL spectra of NGQDs (line a) and NGQDs-apt (line c). Inset of (B): Photographs of NGQDs-apt under visible light and 365 nm UV lamp. (C) TEM and (D) elemental distribution mapping of CdTe QDs@SiO2. (E) UV–vis absorption and (F) FL spectra of CdTe QDs (line a) and CdTe QDs@SiO2 (line b). Inset of (F): Photographs of CdTe QDs@SiO2 under visible light and 365 nm UV lamp.

(A) FL spectra of NGQDs-apt/CdTe QDs@SiO2 system (marked as A, line a), A + ZEN (line b), A + MTX (line c) and A + MTX + ZEN (line d). (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of MTX (line e) and FL spectrum NGQDs-apt/CdTe QDs@SiO2 probes solution (line a). (C) Zeta potentials of CdTe QDs@SiO2 and MTX. (D) The FL lifetime of CdTe QDs@SiO2 in the absence and presence of MTX.

(A) FL spectra of the NGQDs-apt/CdTe QDs@SiO2 system with the addition of ZEN (from top to bottom: 0, 0.32, 0.64, 1.6, 6.4, 32, 64, 320 pg mL−1). (B) Linear calibration curve of the ratiometric FL aptasensor for ZEN detection. (C) FL spectra of the CdTe QDs@SiO2 with the addition of ZEN (from top to bottom: 0, 0.32, 0.64, 1.6, 6.4, 32, 64, 160, 320 pg mL−1). (D) Linear calibration curve of the single-signal FL aptasensor for ZEN detection.
Schematic illustration of the ratiometric FL aptasensor based on competition strategy for ZEN detection.
Results of ZEN detection in corn and barley flour using the developed aptasensor (n = 3) and HPLC-FL standard method.
Statistical comparisons of the developed aptasensor and HPLC-FL standard method for ZEN detection.

head.ce:title: Inner filter effect-modulated ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor based on competition strategy for zearalenone detection in cereal crops: Using mitoxantrone as quencher of CdTe QDs@SiO2

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology, Ministry of Education, School of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology, Ministry of Education, School of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bioengineering, School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 19104, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Engineering and Applied Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bioengineering, School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, PA 19104, United States

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Longgang District Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Shenzhen 518172, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Longgang District Center for Disease Control and Prevention

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Longgang District Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Shenzhen 518172, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, an innovative ratiometric fluorescence (FL) aptasensor was successfully fabricated for the accurate analysis of zearalenone (ZEN) in corn and barley flour. The ZEN aptamer-modified nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs-apt) and silica sphere-encapsulated cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTe QDs@SiO2) were directly mixed and applied as ratiometric probes. In the absence of ZEN, mitoxantrone (MTX), which was innovatively introduced as quencher, was captured by NGQDs-apt and its inner filter effect (IFE) on CdTe QDs@SiO2 was inhibited. When ZEN existed, MTX separated from NGQDs-apt and re-dispersed around CdTe QDs@SiO2 owing to the competitive binding of ZEN with its aptamer. As the IFE of free MTX on CdTe QDs@SiO2 recovering, the FL intensity of CdTe QDs@SiO2 was quenched, while the FL intensity of NGQDs-apt was nearly invariant. On this basis, a ratiometric FL aptasensor for ZEN was fabricated, which exhibited outstanding detection performances with a desirable detection limit of 0.32 pg mL−1.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of nonsteroidal estrogenic mycotoxins, zearalenone (ZEN) is mainly produced by Fusarium graminearum and Fusarium culmorum, and widely found in corn, wheat, barley and sorghum (Jiang et al., 2019; Luo et al., 2019). Due to its reproductive, neurotoxic, and even carcinogenic effects on humans and animals, ZEN has been categorized as the group III carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) (He & Yan, 2020). In order to avoid the incident based on ZEN, many countries have specified the maximum regulation limits of ZEN in different crops and their products, for example, 50 μg kg−1 for cereal-based products in France, 60 μg kg−1 for corn-based and wheat-based products in China, 100 μg kg−1 for cereals in Italy, 60 μg kg−1 for wheat in Australia and so on (Ma et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2018, 2017). Therefore, accurate, sensitive and selective methods are sufficiently required to quantify ZEN concentration in crops.

Among the well-developed methods for ZEN detection, gas and liquid chromatography combined with different detection methods, such as tandem mass spectrometry (Rodríguez-Carrasco et al., 2014), ultraviolet spectrometry (Winter et al., 2008) and fluorescence (FL) spectrometry (Garrido et al., 2013), display high selectivity, precision and reliability. However, complex sample pretreatment, expensive equipment and professional operator are always indispensable. Alternatively, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Dong et al., 2018), electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunoassay (Zheng et al., 2018) and fluorescence immunochromatographic assay (Tang et al., 2017) have been widely established up to now. Among these immunosensing system, antibody is generally adopted as the recognition element to enhance the selectivity. However, the relatively high cost, poor stability and consuming time of antibody limit its application in detection field. Consequently, it is urgently necessary to introduce the recognition element with better performance, like low detection cost, simple experimental process and enhanced stability into the new developed detection strategies.

ZEN-aptamer, a new recognition element for ZEN, was firstly screened out by Chen, Huang et al. (2013). Nowadays, many signal readout methods including electrochemistry (Ji et al., 2019), ECL (Luo et al., 2019), colorimetry (Taghdisi et al., 2018) and FL (Wu et al., 2017) have been developed to selectively and sensitively detect ZEN by introducing ZEN-aptamer. Therein, FL aptasensor possesses comparative advantages such as attractive simplicity, fast response and easy readout (Au et al., 2019; Wei et al., 2020; Zhai et al., 2018), but it is still in its infancy for ZEN detection. For example, Wu et al. (2017) presented a FL aptasensing strategy on basis of upconverting nanoparticles (UCNPs) as a signal generator to detect ZEN. Despite making progress in the selectivity and sensitivity, the single-signal output mode is susceptible to various factors including light scattering, laser source, environment and probe concentration (Wang et al., 2016). On the contrary, the ratiometric FL technique, applying dual-signal output mode, is considered to be competent in conquering aforementioned interference problems via built-in correction, accompanied by the superior accuracy and reliability (Li et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2015). Consequently, constructing a novel ratiometric FL aptasensor with high sensitivity and accuracy for ZEN detection has become one of the important research topics.

To construct ratiometric FL strategy, the ratiometric probes, in which the target-insensitive probe and the target-sensitive probe output reference and response signal at two distinguishable emission wavelengths respectively, are the prerequisite. Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have multifarious functional groups, wide excitation and size-tunable emission spectra, which endow semiconductor QDs ideal signal output probe to develop ratiometric FL sensors (Hua et al., 2015). Currently, combination of semiconductor QDs and organic dyes has been massively reported as ratiometric FL probes for target analysis, but improvements are still needed in terms of reducing toxicity, enhancing stability, and simplifying synthesis process (Wang et al., 2018; Yan et al., 2015). In comparison with the organic dyes, carbon-based QDs are endowed with low toxicity, chemical inertness and stable optical properties, which are assumed to be appropriate cooperator of semiconductor QDs (Liu et al., 2019, 2020). For instance, Chen et al. (2018) integrated carbon dots and cadmium telluride (CdTe) QDs through chemical hybridization to acquire ratiometric probes, based on which a ratiometric FL sensor was fabricated for uranyl ion analysis. Lu et al. (2019) proposed sensitive ratiometric FL strategy on basis of hybridizing CdTe QDs and carbon dots into core–shell structured silica nanoparticles to detect H2O2 and glucose. Nevertheless, the covalent hybridization procedures of ratiometric FL probes potentially cause a great energy loss, accompanied by the reduction of fluorescence efficiency (Liang et al., 2017).

In the present work, without any chemical hybridization, a novel ratiometric FL aptasensor was firstly developed for trace detection of ZEN by directly mixing ZEN-aptamer modified nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs-apt) and silica sphere-encapsulated CdTe QDs (CdTe QDs@SiO2) to output the reference signal and response signal, respectively (Scheme 1 ). Innovatively, mitoxantrone (MTX), which was potentially applied as a quencher of CdTe QDs@SiO2 instead of only being a target, was introduced into the NGQDs-apt/CdTe QDs@SiO2 system. MTX was assembled on NGQDs-apt by intercalating the planar anthraquinone one ring between base pairs and connecting the nitrogen-containing side chains with the negatively charged phosphate groups of ZEN-aptamer (Saljooqi et al., 2019). Therein, the assembly of MTX on NGQDs-apt could be effectively controlled by adjusting the amount of MTX in the system, allowing that the internal filtration effect (IFE) of MTX on CdTe QDs@SiO2 was almost completely hindered. Upon the addition of ZEN, the competitive binding between ZEN and MTX with ZEN-aptamer was triggered, which released MTX from the NGQDs-apt, and further caused the recovered IFE of MTX on CdTe QDs@SiO2. The FL signal of CdTe QDs@SiO2 and NGQDs-apt obviously decreased and slightly changed, respectively, thus ZEN concentration-dependent decrease of the ratiometric signal was obtained.

As described in Supporting Information (SI), the gentle reflux method was adopted to prepare NGQDs according to previous report (Yin et al., 2016). Then, the NGQDs and aptamer were conjugated together via amidation reaction (Liu et al., 2015). Briefly, 400 μL of EDC (1.92 mg mL−1) and NHS (0.58 mg mL−1) was added into 800 μL of NGQDs solution (10 mg mL−1), and the mixture was stirred for 15 min at room temperature. Afterward, 800 μL of amino-modified aptamer (1 μM) were added drop by drop, and the resultant mixture solution was constantly stirred for 2 h. Finally, the NGQDs-apt was stored at 4 °C for use.

The preparation process of CdTe QDs according to previous report was shown in SI (Xu et al., 2017). The CdTe QDs@SiO2 were subsequently prepared by the modified Stὅber method (Hua et al., 2015). Typically, 1 mL of CdTe QDs (10 μM) were mixed with 6 mL of ethanol and stirred for 5 min at room temperature. Following that, 20 μL of APTES was added, and the mixture solution was continuously stirred for 6 h. After 100 μL of NH3·H2O and TEOS were successively added, the polymerization reaction was initiated. Subsequently, the mixture solution was continuously stirred for 24 h. After centrifuging and washing with alcohol and water, the product was stored in powder form at 4 °C.

The 120 μL of NGQDs-apt (4 mg mL−1) and 6 μL of CdTe QDs@SiO2 (1 mg mL−1) were mixed to prepare NGQDs-apt/CdTe QDs@SiO2 probes solution. Thereafter, adding 30 μL of MTX (4.44 μg mL−1), the mixture solution was incubated for 10 min at room temperature. Then, ZEN standardized solutions with different concentration were introduced. After further incubating 40 min, the FL signal of the ratiometric probes were measured under excitation wavelength at 350 nm.

The corn and barley flours were obtained from Zhenjiang local supermarket (Jiangsu Province). As previous reported (Luo et al., 2019), 1 g of samples were adequately dispersed in ZEN standard solution with different concentrations (0, 80, 800 ng mL−1) and then dried at room temperature for 48 h, respectively. Afterward, the processed samples were extracted with 10 mL of methanol–water (6:4, V/V) mixture solution, and then shook for 2 h at 150 rpm. The solutions were centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min and carefully filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane. Finally, the treated solutions were diluted with PBS solution for testing by the developed aptasensor. For comparison, the high-performance liquid chromatography-fluorescence spectrometry (HPLC-FL) method was conducted to prove the accuracy of as-developed aptasensor.

In order to confirm the successful grafting of aptamer with NGQDs (preparation and corresponding characterization are displayed in Fig. S1), Zeta potential experiment were performed firstly. Fig. S2A indicates that ZEN aptamer (-38.2 mV), NGQDs (–22.0 mV), NGQDs-apt (-19.2 mV) are all negatively charged, and the potential value of NGQDs-apt is smallest. Herein, the Zeta potential were directly measured by corresponding instrument (Malvern Nano-ZS Zeta Sizer) based on the Henry equation:
(1)
U
E
=
2 ε z f ( K a )
3 η
where the UE, z, ε, ƒ(Ka) and η represent respectively electrophoretic mobility, Zeta potential, dielectric constant, Henry function and viscosity. According to the previous reports (Jiang et al., 2009; Bhattacharjee, 2016; Qu et al., 2017), the increased mass and size may cause the decrease of electrophoretic mobility. Therefore, we speculate that after combining with each other, the electrophoretic mobility of NGQDs-apt becomes smaller than that of the individual NGQDs and ZEN aptamer due to the increased mass and size. On this basis, the Zeta potential of NGQDs-apt is smallest compared with the individuals, which indirectly proves the successful preparation of NGQDs-apt. Furthermore, the gel electrophoresis experiment was studied. As shown in Fig. S2B, clear bands of both pure ZEN aptamer (lane a) and NGQDs-apt (lane b) can be observed. However, the shift of NGQDs-apt is faster than that of the pure ZEN-aptamer, which should be attributed to the situation that the shape of ZEN aptamer becomes from linear to supercoiled after grafting with NGQDs (Richards and Rodger, 2007). These results prove that NGQDs-apt are successfully synthesized. The optical properties of NGQDs before and after conjugated with ZEN-aptamer were firstly investigated by ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and FL spectrometry. The UV–vis spectrum of the NGQDs-apt (Fig. 1 A, line c) not only exhibits the characteristic peak of NGQDs at 334 nm (Fig. 1A, line a), but also displays an absorbance peak at 259 nm that is ascribed to the base of aptamer (Fig. 1A, line b) (Liu et al., 2015). The result suggests the successful combination of NGQDs and ZEN-aptamer. From inset of Fig. 1B, it can be seen that NGQDs-apt show colorless and bright blue under daylight and 365 nm UV lamp, respectively. Fig. 1B demonstrates that the FL emission of NGQDs at 435 nm changes barely in shape and intensity upon the linking with ZEN-aptamer, illustrating that the NGQDs-apt has excellent stability (Liu et al., 2013), which is beneficial for being as reference probe.

Although CdTe QDs (preparation and corresponding characterization are shown in Fig. S3) possess good optical properties and have been widely used as signal probe in ratiometric FL sensing system, their stability needs to be improved in the NGQDs-apt/CdTe QDs system (Fig. S4A and B). Therefore, silica was employed as nanocarrier to entrap CdTe QDs and overcome the above-mentioned problem. Firstly, the morphology of as-prepared CdTe QDs@SiO2 were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) technology. As can be seen from Fig. 1C, CdTe QDs@SiO2 are homogenous sphere in shape with an average size of 86 nm. The mapping spectra of individual particle (Fig. 1D) demonstrate that Si, O, Cd and Te elements are homogeneously distributed in the CdTe QDs@SiO2 nanosphere, proving the successful encapsulation of CdTe QDs by silica. By measuring the UV–vis absorption and FL spectra, the optical properties of CdTe QDs@SiO2 are further evaluated. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CdTe QDs@SiO2 (Fig. 1E, line b) exhibits a relatively wide peak at 598 nm, which is consistent well with the characteristic peak of CdTe QDs (Fig. 1E, line a). As shown in the inset of Fig. 1F, the colorless solution of CdTe QDs@SiO2 emits bright red fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp. The FL emission peak of CdTe QDs@SiO2 exhibits obvious red-shift from 665 to 687 nm compared with pure CdTe QDs (Fig. 1F), which should be attributed to the changed surface structure of CdTe QDs by being wrapped into silica sphere (Salgueiriño-Maceira et al., 2006). These results are in good agreement with literature reports (Hua et al., 2015; Jing et al., 2011), proving the successful synthesis of CdTe QDs@SiO2. Ultimately, the stability of CdTe QDs@SiO2 were investigated. As shown in Fig. S3C, when CdTe QDs are wrapped into silica, the FL signal of CdTe QDs@SiO2 changes barely because the silica layer prevents CdTe QDs direct contact with the external (Hua et al., 2015). More importantly, the ratiometric signal between CdTe QDs@SiO2 and NGQDs-apt has remained approximately within 12 h (Fig. S4D), indicating that they have good stability and can be applied to develop ratiometric FL sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and mechanism investigation of the ratiometric FL system for ZEN detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to confirm that the detection of ZEN using the developed ratiometric FL aptasensor is feasible, the FL signal of the NGQDs-apt/CdTe QDs@SiO2 probes was measured during the fabrication process. Under single wavelength excitation of 350 nm, the ratiometric probes solution (marked as A) displays two well-resolvable emission peaks that are assigned to NGQDs-apt at 435 nm and CdTe QDs@SiO2 at 687 nm, respectively (Fig. 2 A, line a). With the addition of ZEN (0.32 ng mL−1), the FL signal of the mixture solution is nearly the same as that of the ratiometric probes (Fig. 2A, line b), proving that the ratiometric probes cannot directly detect ZEN. Consequently, we innovatively introduce MTX as quencher of CdTe QDs@SiO2 to construct ratiometric system for ZEN analysis. In the absence of ZEN, MTX was assembled on NGQDs-apt by intercalating the planar anthraquinone one ring between base pairs and connecting the nitrogen-containing side chains with the negatively charged phosphate groups of ZEN-aptamer (Saljooqi et al., 2019). As a result, the quenching effect of MTX on CdTe QDs@SiO2 is hindered, and the FL intensity of CdTe QDs@SiO2 (defined as F687) decreases slightly (Fig. 2A, line c). Nevertheless, the addition of ZEN, which specifically binds with its aptamer, triggers the competitive strategy and induces the release of MTX from the NGQDs-apt. The dispersed MTX around CdTe QDs@SiO2 quenches the FL signal of CdTe QDs@SiO2 by 32% (Fig. 2A, line d). Moreover, it can be obviously observed that the FL intensity of NGQDs-apt (defined as F435) remains unchanged during the construction process, indicating that NGQDs-apt indeed can be used as reference probe to construct ratiometric FL aptasensor. On this basis, the as-developed ratiometric FL aptasensor for ZEN detection is realized by using the ratiometric signal (F687/F435) as quantitative signal, illustrating that the introduction of MTX is indispensable in NGQDs-apt/CdTe QDs@SiO2 system.

To discuss the quenching mechanism of MTX on NGQDs-apt/CdTe QDs@SiO2 system deeply, UV–vis absorption spectrum of MTX and FL spectrum of ratiometric probes were firstly studied. As can be seen from Fig. 2B, the FL emission peak of CdTe QDs@SiO2 (line a) and UV–vis absorption peak of MTX (line e) partially overlap at 670 nm, illustrating the possibility of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and IFE between MTX and CdTe QDs@SiO2 (Chen, Wu et al., 2013; Liang et al., 2017; Peng et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2012). In order to further reveal the quenching mechanism of MTX on CdTe QDs@SiO2, Zeta potential technique was performed, and corresponding results show that both MTX and CdTe QDs@SiO2 are negatively charged (Fig. 2C), which effectively impedes the occurrence of FRET between MTX and CdTe QDs@SiO2 (Zhang et al., 2012). Furthermore, the FL lifetime of CdTe QDs@SiO2 in the absence and presence of MTX was separately measured and calculated to be 28.56 and 28.48 ns (Fig. 2D), further indicating that the quenching mechanism of MTX on CdTe QDs@SiO2 is attributed to IFE rather than FRET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the developed aptasensor for ZEN detection
ce:section.ce:para:
Firstly, the dynamic response range of the ratiometric FL aptasensor for ZEN analysis was estimated. Fig. 3 A shows that the FL signal of CdTe QDs@SiO2 is dynamically quenched with ZEN concentrations increasing, whereas the FL intensity of NGQDs-apt changes hardly. As can be seen from Fig. 3B, the changed ratiometric signal (Id) is proportional versus the logarithm of ZEN concentration. Herein, Id is calculated according to the following equation:
(2)
I
d
=
F
687
/
F
435
(
F
687
/
F
435
)
0
where F687/F435 and (F687/F435)0 represent the FL intensity ratio of CdTe QDs@SiO2 to NGQDs-apt in the presence and absence of ZEN, respectively. The linear regression curve can be fitted as
(3)
I
d
= 0.9232 - 0.1194
log
C
Z E N

The linear range of the ratiometric FL aptasensor is obtained in the range from 0.32 pg mL−1 to 320 pg mL−1, and the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.32 pg mL−1. In order to further prove the superiority of the ratiometric sensing strategy, FL aptasensor based on CdTe QDs@SiO2 as signal probe for ZEN detection under the same conditions was investigated (Fig. 3C). Analogously, the FL intensity of the CdTe QDs@SiO2 constantly decreases with the increase of ZEN concentrations. The changed FL intensity (Is) is linearly correlated with the log CZEN in the range of 6.40–320 pg mL−1 and the poor LOD of 6.40 pg mL−1 (Fig. 3D). Herein, Is is calculated according to the following equation:
(4)
I
s
=
F
687
(
F
687
)
0
where F687 and (F687)0 represent the FL intensity of CdTe QDs@SiO2 with and without ZEN, respectively. Obviously, the ratiometric FL strategy based on F687/F435 possesses one order of magnitude wider linear range and 20 times lower detection limit than that based on the single-signal from CdTe QDs@SiO2. Moreover, the calculated sensitivity of ratiometric FL aptasensor (the linear slope is 0.1194) is higher than that of single-signal aptasensor (the linear slope is 0.0920), which is advantageous for the quantitative analysis of ZEN in complex samples. The superior analytical performance should be attributed to the dual-signal output mode via the utilized NGQDs-apt as reference probe, which has built-in calibration to overcome environmental interference problems and improve accuracy (Wei et al., 2020). More importantly, the introduction of NGQDs-apt not only allows the as-developed ratiometric FL aptasensor to monitor the corresponding ZEN concentration increase and decrease, but also allows quantification of ZEN without the need of high and accurate concentration because absolute fluorescence intensity is not required for ratiometric FL aptasensor.

In addition, the analytical performance of the developed ratiometric FL aptasensor for ZEN detection was compared with other biosensor, and the corresponding results are listed in Table S1. Compared with antibody-based biosensors such as time-resolved FL immunochromatographic assay (TRFICA) (Tang et al., 2017), SPR (Wei et al., 2019), and ECL (Zheng et al., 2018), the as-developed ratiometric FL aptasensor exhibits lower cost, stronger specificity and superior stability because aptamer is applied as a recognition molecule. Although the electrochemical aptasensor (Ma et al., 2019) has the widest linear range, the complicated modification of the electrode is essential. Finally, compared with the FL aptasensor based on single-signal (Niazi et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2017), the designed ratiometric FL sensing strategy has good accuracy and lower detection limit, which is favorable for trace detection of ZEN in real samples.

To further assess the specificity of the proposed ratiometric FL aptasensor for ZEN detection, effect of some potential interferences on the ratiometric signal was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S6, although the concentration of interferences (including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Glucose (Glu), aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), fumonisin B1 (FB1) and ochratoxin A (OTA)) and their mixture (Mix) are 15 times higher than that of ZEN, the FL signal of the NGQDs-apt/CdTe QDs@SiO2 system changes hardly with the addition of the interferences (Fig. S6, columns 1-11). By contrast, the ratiometric signal obviously decreases upon the addition of ZEN owing to the special recognition of ZEN with its aptamer (Fig. S6, columns 12). Moreover, the ratiometric signal change was also measured by adding ZEN and Mix (Fig. S6, columns 13). The result shows that after adding the mixed solution, the ratiometric signal is consistent with that of pure ZEN, which proves the good selectivity of the developed ratiometric FL aptasensor for ZEN analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ZEN in corn and barley flour
ce:section.ce:para: Artificially, ZEN-contaminated corn and barley flour samples were applied to explore the practical application feasibility of the ratiometric FL aptasensor. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries are in the range of 96.9–107.8% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) under 5.23%. Furthermore, the accuracy of the ratiometric FL aptasensor was verified with HPLC-FL standard method. The obtained results are no obvious diversity between the two methods, approving that the developed ratiometric FL aptasensor has great accuracy and reliability.

Moreover, the statistical methods of F-test and t-test were used to analyze the diversity between the developed aptasensor and HPLC-FL. As shown in Table 2 , the calculated F and t are less than the Ftable and ttable, and P values are both beyond 0.05 at a confidence level (CL) of 95%, indicating that no significantly difference between the developed aptasensor and HPLC-FL. The results further illustrate that the fabricated aptasensor for ZEN detection in real samples has good reliability.

In summary, for the first time, CdTe QDs@SiO2 and NGQDs-apt were directly mixed to fabricate an innovative ratiometric FL aptasensor for the reliable and specific detection of ZEN in corn and barley flour. The use of NGQDs-apt, acting as the reference signal generator and inhibitor of the IFE of MTX on CdTe QDs@SiO2, not only simplified the sensing process, but also exhibited improved sensitivity and accuracy in comparison with single-signal FL aptasensor. Furthermore, the proposed ratiometric FL aptasensor was implemented to analyze ZEN in corn and barley flour with well-pleasing recoveries, and the obtained results were in good agreement with that of HPLC-FL standard method, demonstrating its promising practicability in cereal samples.

Xiaoya Bi: Investigation, Data curation, Software, Writing - original draft. Libo Li: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xiaohong Liu: Software, Writing - review & editing. Lijun Luo: Software, Writing - review & editing. Zhiliang Cheng: Writing - review & editing. Jinying Sun: Data curation. Zhibin Cai: Data curation. Jinming Liu: Data curation. Tianyan You: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22074055, 61801195), the Innovation/Entrepreneurship Program of Jiangsu Province, the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (18KJB416004) and the Priority Academic Program Development (PAPD) of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. PAPD-2018-87).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.129171.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
